corrections.text: The article places two phrases in direct quotation marks and attributes them to Judge B. Lynn Winmill 'per the HIPAA Journal': 'vast amounts of essentially non-anonymized information' and 'alleged invasion of privacy...plausibly constitutes a substantial injury to consumers.' These exact phrases do not appear in the HIPAA Journal article cited (hipaajournal.com/ftcs-amended-complaint-against-kochava-survives-motion-to-dismiss/), which paraphrases the ruling without reproducing verbatim court language. The substance of the ruling — that Winmill denied Kochava's motion to dismiss and found the FTC's amended complaint met the plausibility standard — is accurately reported; only the verbatim quotation is unverifiable from the cited source.

The article attributes Roe v. Wade context ('The original 2022 lawsuit was filed in the immediate aftermath of the Supreme Court's decision to overturn Roe v. Wade') to the HIPAA Journal. This context does not appear in the HIPAA Journal article. The correct source for this framing is AdExchanger (adexchanger.com/privacy/the-ftc-bars-kochava-from-selling-sensitive-data-without-consent/), which states: 'In 2022, not long after the Supreme Court's Dobbs decision overturned Roe v. Wade, the FTC sued Kochava.'

The article's summary and Overview describe this as 'the most significant U.S. enforcement action against a location data broker to date, according to Privacy Guides.' This characterization does not appear in the Privacy Guides article (privacyguides.org/news/2026/05/07/ftc-to-ban-data-broker-from-selling-location-data/). It is the contributor's editorial characterization, not a claim sourced from Privacy Guides.
